blocks.text: Re: Market Entry Assessment
Dear Mr. Villegas:

This letter confirms the terms on which Calderwood Partners will carry out a market entry assessment for {{fact:f:E-2012-001.client}}, and it becomes our agreement once you have countersigned it. The question you have put to us is a single one: whether the new market you are weighing is worth entering, and, if it is, on what terms and by which route. It follows our conversations this spring and the internal figures your finance group sent through afterward. Below I set out what we will examine, how we will go about it, who will do the work, and the commercial terms that govern it.
Scope of the Assessment

The assessment answers three questions, in order. The first is how large and how attractive the market actually is: its size today, the pace at which it is growing, and the margins earned by the companies already in it. The second is where you would stand on entering it: which competitors hold which ground, what a customer in that market buys and why, and where a company with your capabilities could win business rather than merely take part in it. The third is how you should enter, if the case supports entering at all: by building from your own base, by acquiring a position, or by partnering into one, each route weighed on cost, on speed, and on risk. You will receive a recommendation, not a set of choices left open.

Some matters sit outside this work and can be taken up separately if you decide you want them. We will size the opportunity and recommend a way into it; we will not negotiate an acquisition, approach a prospective partner in your name, or build the operating plan that a decision to enter would eventually call for. Neither do we advise on the tax or financing structure of any transaction. Where the assessment runs up against a question we have not been engaged to answer, we will say so plainly instead of answering it by assumption.
How We Will Work

We work from your own numbers and from the market as it stands, not from a template carried in from some other engagement. Work begins on {{fact:f:E-2012-001.start}}, and we expect the assessment to run about three months from that point. In the opening weeks we build the market picture from published data, from industry sources, and from interviews we conduct ourselves, and we set it beside your internal figures so that every number we later put in front of you can be traced to one your own people already recognize. From that base we frame the entry options and test each of them before it reaches you as a recommendation. You will see our thinking as it develops; nothing of consequence will arrive as a surprise at the end.

The work depends on timely access to your internal figures and to the people who understand them. We will send a specific, itemized data request in the first week and will keep it as short as the questions allow. Where a figure is hard to produce in the form we ask for, tell us, and we will find another route to it instead of holding up the schedule waiting on it.
Who Will Do the Work

Jason Torres, Principal, leads the assessment and is your point of contact for scope, schedule, and findings; he is the one to call when a question will not keep. James Green, Senior Associate, carries the data and the analysis, and is the person your finance staff will deal with directly on the numbers. Deborah Gordon, Associate, leads the synthesis and the drafting of what we conclude. I review the analysis and the recommendation before either reaches you, and I am accountable to you for the quality of both.
Fees and Payment

Our fee for the assessment is {{fact:f:E-2012-001.fee}}, fixed for the scope set out above. We invoice monthly for the work performed in the preceding month, and each invoice falls due within thirty days of its date. Reasonable travel and out-of-pocket costs are billed at cost and shown separately from the fee. Work beyond this scope is not begun on assumption; should you ask us for something this letter does not cover, we will price it and agree it with you before we start.
Term and Termination

This agreement takes effect on the date you sign below and continues until we deliver the final assessment and you have settled the final invoice. Either party may end it on thirty days' written notice to the other. On termination you will pay us for the work performed and the expenses incurred through the effective date, and we will hand over our work product as it then stands, together with a short written account of where the assessment had reached.
Confidentiality

We will hold the information you give us in confidence and use it only for this assessment. Access is limited to the team named above and to those in our office whose work requires it. We will not disclose your information outside the firm without your written consent, save where a court or a regulator with jurisdiction compels it, and in that event we will tell you first if we are permitted to. The obligation continues after the engagement has ended.
Limitation of Liability

Our liability to you arising out of this engagement, whether framed in contract or in tort, will not exceed the total fees you pay us under it, except in the case of our fraud or willful misconduct. Neither party will be liable to the other for indirect or consequential loss, including lost profits, whether or not the possibility was made known. Our conclusions rest on the accuracy and completeness of the information you provide and of the market data available to us; we will test what we reasonably can, but we do not audit either.
Governing Law

This agreement is governed by the laws of the state in which our principal office is located, without regard to its conflict-of-laws rules. Should a dispute arise, senior representatives of the two sides will attempt to settle it in discussion before either party begins proceedings. This letter is the entire agreement between us on its subject, and it may be amended only in a writing signed by both.

If these terms match your understanding, please countersign both copies, keep one for your records, and return the other to me. If any of them does not, telephone me and we will settle it before you sign. We are glad to be taking on this work, and the team looks forward to getting started with your people at Garcia-Werner.
Very truly yours,
To: Jason Torres, Deborah Gordon
From: James Green
Re: {{fact:f:E-2012-001.client}} market entry assessment, kickoff and the first weeks

We are signed, and the market entry assessment for {{fact:f:E-2012-001.client}} starts on {{fact:f:E-2012-001.start}}. The objective is narrow enough to state in a line: tell the client whether the new market is worth entering, and, if it is, by which route and at what cost. Three months, one recommendation, built on the client's own figures and on the market as far as we can document it. Our single contact is Mark Villegas, the Chief Financial Officer, and everything that goes to the client goes through him.

The client already serves a related market and does well in it. The real question is whether the adjacent one is different enough to reward the effort of entering, or similar enough that the same playbook carries over. That distinction is what the whole assessment turns on, and it is the one Mark Villegas keeps coming back to. Keep it in front of you when the data starts to pile up.

A word on scope before the work spreads. We size the market and recommend a way in; we do not negotiate an acquisition, sound out partners, or write the operating plan that entry would need later. If any of that is asked for while we are on site, the answer is that we will scope it, not that we have begun it. Keep the work to the assessment we were hired for.
Lines of Work

items: Market size and attractiveness. I pull the published and industry data and build the picture of the market's size, its growth, and the margins in it. Where two sources disagree, the gap gets flagged in the first pass, not smoothed over later.
Competitive ground. We map who holds which position, and where a company with the client's cost base could actually win business rather than merely enter the market.
Customer demand. What a buyer in this market purchases, and why, from the interviews and whatever demand data we can get our hands on.
Entry options. Jason Torres frames build, buy, and partner, each sized on cost, speed, and risk, so the recommendation rests on a comparison and not on a preference.
Synthesis and recommendation. Deborah Gordon owns the write-up. Nothing reaches the client as a recommendation before the analysis behind it holds together.
Client cadence. A short status goes to Mark Villegas on a steady beat, so the client is never left guessing where the work stands.

blocks.text: The First Weeks

items: I draft the data request this week, itemized and with the field definitions attached, and Jason Torres reviews it before it goes to Mark Villegas.
We agree the interview list, competitors' customers, trade sources, and the client's own commercial staff, before we ask anyone for a calendar slot.
Deborah Gordon sets up the synthesis structure now, so findings land in a frame that already exists instead of one thrown together at the end.
I will circulate a one-page work plan with the milestones for the three months by early next week, so we are all working to the same calendar.
No market size and no entry cost gets quoted to anyone at Garcia-Werner until the underlying data reconciles.

blocks.text: By the end of the week I want the data request out to Mark Villegas and the interview list agreed among the three of us. With both settled, the first month opens cleanly, and we are not chasing access once the interviews have started.
Mark Villegas, with Jason Torres copied:

Thank you for the finance extracts your team sent last week. They are loaded, and they reconcile cleanly against the figures we pulled from the published sources for {{fact:f:E-2012-001.client}}. The market sizing is taking shape, and Deborah Gordon has the synthesis frame far enough along that findings will have somewhere to land as they come in.

A couple of things from our side would help us keep to schedule. We are still missing the regional sales breakdown by segment for the last full year; the summary you sent is close, but the segment cut is what lets us separate the market you are weighing from the one you already serve. Separately, the customer interviews we discussed will need a name and an introduction from someone at Garcia-Werner, since a cold approach in this market rarely earns a straight answer.

Jason Torres will be in your offices for the site visit we set, and I will send the interview times as soon as the contact is in hand. If the segment breakdown is easier to pull in a rougher form, send that and we will work from it instead of waiting on a clean file. Nothing further is needed from you today.
James Green
Working Session: Market Sizing and Entry Options

Minutes recorded by Deborah Gordon. Subject: {{fact:f:E-2012-001.client}} market entry assessment. Working session held with the client. Purpose of the session was to review the completed market sizing, the competitive map, and the three entry routes now under study. No decisions on entry were sought or taken. The meeting ran to time.
Attendees

items: James Green, Senior Associate
Jason Torres, Principal
Deborah Gordon, Associate
Mark Villegas, Chief Financial Officer, Garcia-Werner

blocks.text: Market Sizing

James Green presented the size and growth estimates. The published sources and the client's segment data now agree within a narrow band. The target market is the larger of the two under discussion. Growth in the target segment runs ahead of the client's current markets. The margin profile is comparable to the client's existing business. Two data gaps remain. One is a missing prior-year figure. One is a definitional mismatch between two sources. Both are small. Neither changes the current picture. Mark Villegas accepted the sizing as a working baseline. James Green noted that later refinements will not reopen it.
Competitive Position

Jason Torres reviewed the competitive map. Three incumbents hold most of the volume. None is dominant. Pricing across the three is close. A gap sits in the mid-tier, between the volume players and the specialists. The client's cost position would be an advantage there. Mark Villegas named a fourth competitor absent from the data. It is a recent entrant and small. Jason Torres will add it and check whether it changes the mid-tier read. The team does not expect it to.
Entry Options

Deborah Gordon summarized the three routes under review: build, acquire, and partner. No route has been recommended. Each needs a cost and a timeline before the comparison holds. The build route is the slowest and the most within the client's control. The acquire route is the fastest and the least certain on price. The partner route sits between them. Mark Villegas asked that the acquire route carry a rough valuation range. The team agreed to scope one. The full comparison comes to the next working session.
Client Input

Mark Villegas confirmed the segment definition in use. He flagged one customer relationship that may open interview access. Deborah Gordon logged it for follow-up. Mark Villegas asked for the option comparison in writing ahead of the next session. The team agreed. No change was made to scope or timeline.
Next Session

The next working session will take up the full comparison of the three routes and the valuation range for the acquire route. Date to be confirmed with Mark Villegas. The team will circulate the written comparison in advance. No recommendation will be tabled until the comparison is complete.
Action Items

rows: Close the two remaining gaps in the market sizing
Add the fourth competitor to the map and re-check the mid-tier
Scope a rough valuation range for the acquire route
Confirm the customer interview contact and set the schedule
Circulate the entry-options comparison in writing

blocks.text: Status Report: Market Entry Assessment

Prepared for the office of the Chief Financial Officer, {{fact:f:E-2012-001.client}}, by the engagement team. This report covers the period since our last update and sets out where the assessment stands at its midpoint.
Summary

The assessment for {{fact:f:E-2012-001.client}} is at its midpoint and on plan. The market picture is built and has been agreed with your team as a working baseline. The competitive and demand work is largely complete. The entry options are framed and are moving into direct comparison. Against the fixed fee of {{fact:f:E-2012-001.fee}} for the engagement, roughly half the budget is committed, which matches the share of the work delivered. We expect to hold both the schedule and the fee to the plan set at the outset.

In short, the diagnostic half of the engagement is behind us, and the picture is consistent from every direction we have tested it. The market is real and growing, your cost position gives you somewhere to compete, and the demand work supports both. What remains is the decision the assessment was commissioned to inform: not whether the market exists, but whether and how you should enter it, and at what cost and return. The second half is built to answer exactly that.
Completed This Period

The first half of the assessment was spent establishing the facts the recommendation will rest on. That work is now substantially done, and the results have held up against your own figures wherever the two can be compared. We have deliberately front-loaded the diagnostic work so that the second half is spent on judgment rather than data-gathering, and so that any figure we put in front of you in the final report has already been reconciled once against a source your own team recognizes.

items: Market sizing is closed. The size, growth, and margin profile of the target market are established, and they reconcile with your segment data within a narrow band.
The competitive map is complete. The positions of the leading incumbents are documented, along with a gap in the mid-tier where your cost position would carry an advantage.
The first round of customer interviews is finished. Demand in the target segment reads more clearly than the published data alone had suggested, and it points the same way as the sizing.
The three entry routes, build, acquire, and partner, are framed on a common basis of cost, speed, and risk, and are ready for comparison.
Your finance and commercial teams have been responsive throughout, and the data flow has kept pace with the analysis, which is the main reason the first half held to schedule.

blocks.text: The Second Half

The remaining work turns the facts into a recommendation and sizes what acting on it would take. The emphasis now shifts from gathering to judgment, and from the market in general to your position in it specifically. We will bring the comparison to a working session before it is final, so that the recommendation reaches you already tested against your own reading of the market rather than delivered cold.

items: Complete the comparison of the three routes and settle on a recommended way in.
Size the recommended route: what entry would cost, what it would return, and over what period.
Deborah Gordon assembles the analysis and the recommendation into the final assessment.
Hold a working session to take you and your team through the draft before it is finalized.

blocks.text: Risks and Watch Items

Three items bear watching in the second half. None is off track today, and each is noted here so there are no surprises later. The valuation range for the acquisition route rests on comparable-transaction data that is thin in this market; we will present the range with its uncertainty marked, and will not imply a precision the data does not support. Two customer interviews slipped and have been rescheduled; if they slip again, the demand read on one segment stays softer than the rest, and we are managing that with the help of your commercial team at Garcia-Werner. The segment breakdown we requested is still outstanding in its final form, and we are working from the summary in the meantime, which is adequate for now but not for the final numbers. Taken together these are the ordinary uncertainties of an assessment at this stage, not signs of trouble, and we will keep each in view as the numbers firm up.
Where This Leaves Us

Nothing in the work to date changes the plan agreed at kickoff, and the assessment remains on schedule and within budget. The next report will carry the completed comparison and a recommended route, ahead of the working session where we will walk your team through it. Questions on the analysis are best directed to Jason Torres; questions on the underlying numbers to James Green.
